english: for black ink, mtsál-koṅ (mtshal kong) for red ink, vermilion; *lúg-koṅ* (*lug kong*) casting-mould (
('a gathering'?) 1. lag-kód (lag kod) Ld. an armful of corn, a sheaf. 2. affix = kad (kad), ka (ka), ko (ko): *ñyis-kód* (*nyis kod*), *ṅa-kód* (*nga kod*) all the two, all the five Ld. .

root denoting anything round or concave, hence: kor-kór (kor kor) 1. adj. round, circular C. (= *kyir-kyir* (*kyir kyir*) W.); roundish, globular C.; concave, deep, as a soup-plate (opp. to flat) W. 2. sbst. a thick loaf of bread, (opp. to a flat, thin cake) C.; a pan, saucepan W.; a hollow in the ground, a pit not very deep W.; stód-kor (stod kor) a little circle above a letter, Ssk. anuswara; klád-kor (klad kor) id., a dot, zero, naught; ḥdúb-kor ('dub kor) bracelet Cs.; pád-kor (pad kor) a certain way of folding the fingers, so as to represent the form of a lotus-flower; ḥód-kor ('od kor) a radiant circle Cs. Cf. skór-ba (skor ba), ḥkór-ba ('khor ba), ko-ré (ko re).
= ko-ko (ko ko); kos-snyúṅ (kos snyung) with a pointed chin Sch.
also kyag-kyág (kyag kyag), thick, run into clots Cs.

curved, crooked; *go kyag-kyóg co-ce* (*go kyag kyog co ce*) W. to shake one's head, viz. slowly, in meditating; *ri-mo kyag-(ga-) kyog-(gé)* (*ri mo kyag ga kyog ge*) a flourish (in writing) W. Cf. kyog-kyóg (kyog kyog).

I. adj., also kyaṅ-kyaṅ (kyang kyang), kyaṅ-po (kyang po), straight, slender, as a stick; *kyaṅ-kyaṅ riṅ-mo* (*kyang kyang ring mo*) tall, slender, as a man, a tree etc. W. - II. adv. = yaṅ (yang), too, also, always used enclitically, after the letters g, d, b, s.
also *kyaṅ-ṅa-kyoṅ-ṅé* (*kyang nga kyong nge*), indolent, lazy, idle W.; *kyaṅ-kyoṅ co-ce* (*kyang kyong co ce*) to lounge, to be idle W.
also kyar-po (kyar po) flat, not globular Cs.
also *kyar-ra-kyor-ré* (*kyar ra kyor re*), still feeble, as convalescents after a disease.

1. joke, jest, in words (Lś. ku-rei tsig (ku re'i tshig)). - 2. jocular trick, ku-re daṅ kyál-kai pyir (ku re dang kyal ka'i phyir) by way of jest, for fun. - 3. any worthless, foolish, indecent talk Stg.
Lex. w. e.; Sch.: kyal-kyal-ba (kyal kyal ba) to go round (?).
= kyar-kyór (kyar kyor), dúd-gro kyal-kyól ga (dud gro kyal kyol ga) some poor ill-conditioned beast, speaking of cattle, Mil. nt.

affix I. to sbst.-roots, ending in d, b, s: sign of the genitive case. - II. to verbal roots, after the same final letters, and then without an essential difference from kyis (kyis), to which we add in this place also examples of the other terminations gi(s) (gi(s)), gyi(s) (gyi(s)), yi(s) (yi(s)), i (i) (the s by itself is not used after verbal roots): a. in the sense of a gerund, meaning by (doing something), because, dgós-kyis ḥdóṅ-ṅo (dgos kyis 'dong ngo) we come because it is necessary . . . , or more freq. though, dgaí (dga'i) though she is glad . . . Dzl., in which case it may often be rendered in English by but: she is glad, but . . . ; zas bzáṅ-po mi ḥdód-kyis ṭa-mál-pa zos (zas bzang po mi 'dod kyis tha mal pa zos) he did not care for dainties, but ate vulgar food Dzl.; or it has to be omitted: bdén-pa yin-gyis rdzún-pa ma yin (bden pa yin gyis rdzun pa ma yin) it is true, no fiction Dzl. - b. as an adjective, forming, like kyiṅ (kying) (q. v.), with ḥdug ('dug) or yod (yod) a periphrastical present tense e. g. ḥgroí ḥdug ('gro'i 'dug) he is walking, ḥóṅ-gi yod ('ong gi yod) he is coming. - c. at the end of a sentence in the sense of a finite verb and more particularly in the 1. pers. fut.: ḥgyod mi rmoi ('gyod mi rmo'i) I shall not make you suffer for it Dzl., ṅas grogs byá-yis (ngas grogs bya yis) I shall help Glr., bźag-gi (bzhag gi), and: bźág-gis (bzhag gis) I shall put Glr. This use of kyi(s) (kyi(s)) is said to be quite common at the present time in C., whereas in W. not only the whole gerundial use, but even the distinction of kyi (kyi), gyi (gyi), gi (gi) in the genitive case of a sbst. has disappeared from colloquial language, instead of which the last consonant is repeated and the vowel
